draft.introduction: Archimedes’ Sand Reckoner develops a number system capable of expressing quantities as large as the number of grains of sand that would fill the universe. It also preserves Aristarchus’s heliocentric hypothesis, the earliest known.

translation.text: There are some, King Gelon, who think that the number of the sand is infinite in multitude. Others think that, though not infinite, no number has been named which is great enough to exceed its multitude. But I will try to show you that among the numbers I have named, some exceed the number of grains of sand filling a sphere the size of the universe.
translation.original_text: Τινὲς οἴονται βασιλεῦ Γέλων τὸν τᾶς ψάμμου ἀριθμὸν ἄπειρον εἶμεν τῷ πλήθει

translator_notes.note: Archimedes mentions that Aristarchus of Samos proposed the sun, not the earth, as the center of the planetary system—eighteen centuries before Copernicus. The Sand Reckoner’s exponential number system also anticipates modern scientific notation.
